title: Survey existing agent-sandbox tooling (kubernetes-sigs/agent-sandbox, agent-infra/sandbox, e2b.dev)
description:
Deep-dive comparative investigation of three open-source agent-sandboxing tools, in light of the claude-config Phase 1+ implementation plan.

## Tools to investigate

- https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/agent-sandbox — Kubernetes-based agent sandboxing
- https://github.com/agent-infra/sandbox — Docker-based agent sandboxing
- https://e2b.dev/ — hosted code-execution sandbox for AI agents

## Methodology

Three Opus general-purpose research agents in parallel, one per package. Each writes a focused report to `.tasks/40s.12-sandbox-tooling-survey/<package-slug>.md` covering:

- Architecture and isolation mechanism (containers / VMs / namespaces / unikernels?)
- Feature set and capabilities (filesystem isolation, network policy, credential model, lifecycle, multi-session, observability)
- Operational model (self-hosted vs hosted; deployment surface; runtime requirements)
- Authentication and credential handling
- Differences from claude-config's plan (DEC-006 allow-list, DEC-007 bwrap, DEC-008 tiered credentials, DEC-009/010, ROADMAP Phases 1–7)
- Adoption/integration feasibility for this user's specific use case (single workstation, WSL2, BOCO context, host-tooling-integrated workflow)

After agent reports return, synthesize a comparative report at `.tasks/40s.12-sandbox-tooling-survey/SUMMARY.md`.

## Final deliverable: SUMMARY.md

Four required sections:

1. **Conclusions on scope-reduction opportunities.** Whether any of these apps represent an opportunity to adopt upstream technology and reduce claude-config implementation scope. Concrete recommendations: which DECs/beads would be re-scoped, what's saved, what's lost.
2. **Follow-up questions raised by the research.**
3. **Related technologies to investigate** (synthesizer's identification, informed by what agents surface): e.g., Firecracker, Kata Containers, sandbox-exec, gVisor, Apple Containerization, sysbox.
4. **One-line value-proposition assessment.** Where claude-config sits in the wider open-source AI-sandboxing landscape, in light of these findings.

## Acceptance

- Three per-package reports persisted at `.tasks/40s.12-sandbox-tooling-survey/`
- SUMMARY.md synthesized with the four required sections
- If scope-reduction opportunities identified, follow-up beads filed and relevant DECs cross-referenced for amendment
- Decision recorded in the bead's closing note: adopt / hybridize / stay course
close_reason: Stay course. bwrap remains Phase 1 primitive (DEC-007 unchanged). All three surveyed tools (kubernetes-sigs/agent-sandbox, agent-infra/sandbox, e2b.dev) address an orthogonal problem: 'spawn isolated runtimes for agent outputs in a SaaS/cluster context.' claude-config addresses 'wrap an interactive Claude Code session with host-integrated tooling on a workstation' — distinct niche, ~zero overlap. One Phase 7 architecture refinement filed as ClaudeConfig-tpn.1: design the executor as a multi-backend dispatcher with E2B and AIO Sandbox as candidate backends for untrusted-code-execution and browser/computer-use capability classes. One Phase 1 follow-up filed as ClaudeConfig-40s.13: investigate Claude Code's own sandboxing primitives. SUMMARY.md and per-tool reports persisted at .tasks/40s.12-sandbox-tooling-survey/.

title: Evaluate bubblewrap as Phase 1 sandbox primitive (allow-list policy)
description:
Evaluate bubblewrap (`bwrap`, github.com/containers/bubblewrap) as the Phase 1 sandbox primitive, replacing the planned `unshare --mount` + `sudo -u claude-session` + shadow-tmpfs approach.

## Why we're considering this

Discovered 2026-05-05 while drafting `sandbox/profiles/default.yaml` for ClaudeConfig-40s.4: a deny-list of credential paths (shadow-tmpfs each sensitive directory) is brittle and tail-risky. New tools install new credential paths constantly (`~/.cargo/credentials.toml`, `~/.git-credentials`, `~/.local/share/keyrings/`, `~/.terraform.d/credentials.tfrc.json`, future-tool-X). A miss in the list is a permanent credential leak.

The fix is a policy flip: shadow-mount the entire `~/` (or a wider scope) by default, then bind-mount BACK only the paths the active profile permits. Allow-list, not deny-list. Same kernel mechanism (mount namespaces); opposite default.

Bubblewrap implements that policy natively:
- Allow-list semantics by default (`--bind <src> <dst>` adds; nothing else visible)
- Single binary, no daemon, no images, no registry
- Doesn't require root (uses user namespaces) — likely obviates the separate `claude-session` user account and sudoers rule entirely
- Battle-tested (Flatpak's sandbox runtime)
- Available in every distro's package manager

## Acceptance for this evaluation

- Build a minimal proof-of-concept `claude-sandbox` wrapper using `bwrap` that runs `claude -p "echo hello"` successfully against the canonical `~/.claude/` config.
- Verify allow-list property: a credential path NOT explicitly bound in (e.g., `~/.password-store/`) is invisible inside the sandbox.
- Verify memory write-back: a `bd remember` from inside the sandbox lands in `~/.claude/projects/<hash>/`.
- Verify Anthropic OAuth works (the bind-mounted `~/.claude/.credentials.json` authenticates correctly).
- Compare the bwrap implementation cost / surface area against the unshare-based design previously sketched.
- Decide: adopt bwrap as Phase 1 primitive (and which other Phase 1 children are obviated), or fall back to unshare with policy-flipped defaults.

## Affected Phase 1 children (must wait on this evaluation)

- ClaudeConfig-40s.1 (claude-sandbox wrapper script) — wrapper shape changes substantially
- ClaudeConfig-40s.2 (privileged setup + sudoers) — likely unnecessary under bwrap user namespaces
- ClaudeConfig-40s.4 (default profile + ACLs) — YAML structure changes; ACLs may be unnecessary
- ClaudeConfig-40s.5 (Makefile install target) — install map changes
- J121-ft3 (claude-session user provisioning) — likely unnecessary

## Decisions to record

- DEC-006: allow-list policy as the governing principle for sandbox visibility (regardless of the bwrap-vs-unshare outcome).
- DEC-007 (only if bwrap is adopted): bubblewrap as Phase 1 sandbox primitive.

## Non-goals

- Don't evaluate Docker/Podman/systemd-nspawn here. Containers add host-tooling integration cost and per-invocation startup latency that's poorly suited to wrapping every `claude` invocation. Tradeoffs already explored 2026-05-05.
close_reason: Evaluation complete. bwrap validated against all four acceptance criteria (allow-list, OAuth, memory write-back, env hygiene). Decision recorded in DEC-007: adopt bwrap as Phase 1 sandbox primitive. Reference POC persisted at .tasks/40s.7-bwrap-eval/poc-wrapper.sh. Two open follow-ups (user-model decision, New Relic telemetry compliance) flagged in DEC-007 for separate evaluation.

title: Initial default profile YAML + ACLs on ~/.claude/projects/
description: Reference default sandbox profile (sandbox/profiles/default.yaml) — baseline visibility, no extra paths exposed. ACLs on ~/.claude/projects/<hash>/ granting claude-session write access (set up here, verified again in Phase 4).
notes:
Re-scoped 2026-05-05 per DEC-007. Original scope included ACLs for ~/.claude/projects/<hash>/ — under bwrap with same-UID, no ACLs are needed (sandbox runs as the user). If the user-model follow-up adopts a separate UID (path A), ACLs return to scope and J121-ft3 is reanimated. Until then, this bead delivers only the profile YAML (allow-list shape per DEC-006).
Re-expanded 2026-05-05 per DEC-007 follow-up #1 resolution (A2 adopted). ACL portion is back in scope; the profile YAML now references the bound paths under both hactar and claude-session homes. Implementation: (a) sandbox/profiles/default.yaml with read_only/read_write bind specs + UID/GID for the sandbox user, (b) sandbox/scripts/setup-claude-session-acls.sh providing setfacl-R + default-ACL idempotent setup, invoked by J121-ft3's provisioning script.

title: Makefile install target wired up to deploy Phase 1 components
description: Per DEC-004: marker-block managed for ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, three-way prompt for ~/.claude/settings.json, direct install for /usr/local/{bin,sbin}/, /etc/sudoers.d/, ~/.config/claude-sandbox/profiles/. Timestamped backup before any modification. Idempotent re-runs. make uninstall restores backups.
notes:
Install map narrowed 2026-05-05 per DEC-007. No /etc/sudoers.d/ entry, no /usr/local/sbin/ privileged helper. Just /usr/local/bin/claude-sandbox, ~/.config/claude-sandbox/profiles/*.yaml, and the marker-block update to ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md. If the user-model follow-up reintroduces a separate user, useradd + ACL setup re-enter the install steps.
Install map re-expanded 2026-05-05 per A2 user-model. Steps: (a) /usr/local/bin/claude-sandbox wrapper (direct), (b) ~/.config/claude-sandbox/profiles/*.yaml (direct), (c) ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md marker block (managed), (d) one-time provisioning hook calling J121-ft3's script (useradd + subuid + ACLs). NO /etc/sudoers.d/ entry, NO /usr/local/sbin/ privileged helper.

title: Privileged namespace setup script + sudoers rule
description: Root-only namespace setup at sandbox/sbin/claude-sandbox-priv with NOPASSWD sudoers rule at sandbox/etc/sudoers.d/claude-sandbox. Owns the unshare --mount invocation and shadow-tmpfs mounts of credential paths. Must live in /usr/local/sbin/ (root-owned, user-non-writable) per Common Pitfall #1.
notes: Deferred 2026-05-05 per DEC-007. bwrap is rootless; sudoers + privileged helper are not needed under same-UID. May re-emerge in modified form if user-model follow-up adopts a separate UID via real account. See DEC-007 open follow-up #1.

title: claude-sandbox wrapper script
description: User-invokable entry-point at sandbox/bin/claude-sandbox. Calls sudo /usr/local/sbin/claude-sandbox-priv for privileged namespace setup, then drops to claude-session and execs claude. Single-profile, single-worktree, single-namespace per invocation.
notes:
Implementation shape clarified 2026-05-05 per DEC-007. Wrapper is a thin shell over bwrap (~25 lines of bind specifications). Reference POC at .tasks/40s.7-bwrap-eval/poc-wrapper.sh. Production version reads the bound profile YAML (40s.4) instead of inlining the bind list, and conditionally drops UID if the user-model follow-up adopts a separate user.
Implementation shape narrowed 2026-05-05 per DEC-007 + A2 user-model. Wrapper invocation gains --uid <claude-session-uid> --gid <claude-session-gid> (UIDs read from a static install-time config or /etc/passwd lookup). Cross-user binds: --ro-bind hactar's ~/.claude/{skills,agents,commands,settings.json} → /home/claude-session/.claude/{...}; --bind hactar's ~/.claude/projects/ → /home/claude-session/.claude/projects/ (writable via ACL); --bind /home/claude-session/.claude/.credentials.json (claude-session's own OAuth).

title: Phase 1: claude-sandbox wrapper
description:
Single-namespace, single-profile, single-worktree sandbox wrapper. Universal sandboxing for serial use. See docs/ROADMAP.md#phase-1.

Acceptance:
- claude-sandbox --version confirms wrapper + namespace + claude
- claude-sandbox -p "echo hello" succeeds
- Inside session: credential paths are empty (shadow-tmpfs), env shows no SNOWFLAKE_PRIVATE_KEY/JIRA_API_TOKEN, ~/.claude/ is read-only bind mount, ~/Source/claude-config/ is writable worktree

title: Provision claude-session user + /etc/subuid + ACLs (A2 user-model)
description:
One-shot provisioning script that creates the claude-session user and sets ACLs on ~/.claude/projects/<hash>/ for write-back from sandboxed sessions.

Lives in this repo at sandbox/scripts/provision-claude-session.sh and is invoked by the Makefile install target (see DEC-004). The privileged-namespace setup script lives elsewhere (sandbox/sbin/claude-sandbox-priv); this bead covers user creation + ACLs only.

Acceptance:
- claude-session user exists with appropriate UID/GID
- ACLs grant claude-session write access to ~/.claude/projects/<hash>/
- Idempotent re-runs do not duplicate state
- Idempotent backout supported (make uninstall removes the user + ACLs cleanly)
notes:
Deferred 2026-05-05 per DEC-007. bwrap with same-UID inside the namespace makes a separate claude-session user account unnecessary as a structural requirement. May re-emerge in modified form if user-model follow-up adopts a separate UID via real account (path A). See DEC-007 open follow-up #1.
Revived 2026-05-05 per DEC-007 follow-up #1 + user direction (path A2: rootless bwrap + named claude-session UID via subuid). Implementation:
1. useradd -r -d /home/claude-session -s /usr/sbin/nologin claude-session (system account, no shell)
2. /etc/subuid line granting hactar the right to map to claude-session's UID range
3. /etc/subgid analog for GID
4. setfacl -R -m u:claude-session:rwx ~/.claude/projects/ + default ACL for new files
5. mkdir -p /home/claude-session/.claude/ owned by claude-session for OAuth + .claude.json
6. Documented first-run ceremony: claude-session does its own browser OAuth flow on first sandbox invocation
Idempotent. make uninstall reverses all of it.

title: Investigate Claude Code's built-in sandboxing primitives
description:
Surfaced by ClaudeConfig-40s.12. Open question worth resolving early: what does Claude Code itself ship in terms of sandboxing primitives, and how does that overlap with what we're building?

## Why this matters

If Claude Code already isolates tool-use (Bash command execution, file reads/writes, network) at the application layer, some of what claude-config plans to build at the OS layer may be partially redundant, or composable, or — potentially — duplicative. We should understand the upstream surface before continuing to invest in the OS-layer wrapper.

Specifically worth understanding:
- Bash tool: does Claude Code constrain what commands run, with what env? Are deny rules enforced at the Anthropic side or locally?
- File tool: any path restrictions baked in? Or is it purely the local filesystem's view?
- Network: any Claude-Code-side constraints, or all delegated to OS / DNS / firewall?
- The 50-subcommand bypass disclosure (CVE-2026-25723 family) — what's the post-fix model? Is there a structured policy engine in Claude Code now?
- Anthropic's Computer Use reference implementation (Docker-based) — what does it sandbox? Is the design pattern relevant for our Phase 7 dispatcher?

## Acceptance

- Findings written to .tasks/<this-bead-id>-claude-code-sandboxing-survey/REPORT.md
- If material overlap with claude-config's plan is found, file follow-up beads to re-scope
- If gap is identified that Anthropic addresses, note it as a "rely on upstream" decision rather than reimplementing

## Effort estimate

Low — should be 30-60 min of doc skimming and source/release-notes review, NOT a deep code-level investigation. The goal is "do we need to build less because Claude Code already does X?", not a full audit.

title: Escape-hatch: share-credential.sh + optional GPG encryption
description:
Implement the escape-hatch mechanism for sharing user-owned credentials with `claude-session` when a credential cannot be cleanly issued to a separate principal at the upstream service.

## Design (per discussion 2026-05-05)

### Primary mechanism: `share-credential.sh` (option a)

Static copy with optional GPG encryption-at-rest:

```bash
sandbox/scripts/share-credential.sh <hactar-source-path> <claude-session-relative-target> [--encrypt]
```

Steps:
1. Copy <source> to /home/claude-session/<target>
2. chown claude-session:claude-session <target>
3. chmod 600 <target>
4. (optional --encrypt) GPG-encrypt to claude-session's public key; write .gpg file; remove plaintext copy

claude-session's GPG key should be signed by hactar's personal key — the signature IS the audit attestation that hactar deliberately authorized claude-session to hold this credential.

### Escape-hatch-to-the-escape-hatch: option (b)

For credentials that rotate frequently and externally (cloud SSO refresh, pass-managed entries that change behind the scenes), option (b) — bind-mount from hactar's home with ACL — is available as a documented exception. Profile YAML accepts a `shared_credentials.bind_mounts: [...]` field (empty by default; entries trigger setfacl on hactar's source file granting claude-session read access).

## Friction enforcement

Per DEC-008: every tier-1 credential addition requires a DECISION_LOG entry. Shared credentials are tier-1-by-shared-mechanism; the DEC entry must justify why the credential cannot be issued to a separate principal at the upstream service. This is the structural friction.

## Acceptance

- sandbox/scripts/share-credential.sh implemented (option a + optional GPG)
- sandbox/profiles/SCHEMA.md documents the `shared_credentials.bind_mounts:` field (option b escape-hatch-to-the-escape-hatch)
- Documented usage example for the Jira interim case (sibling bead)
- DEC-NNN records the escape-hatch policy formally; cross-referenced from DEC-008
notes: Filed as DEC-010 (escape-hatch mechanism). The bead's original description said 'DEC-NNN'; that's now resolved to DEC-010.

title: Provision claude-session New Relic ingest key (via Erick)
description:
Request a New Relic ingest license key issued to a `claude-session`-named principal (or BOCO equivalent of a service identity), separate from hactar's personal NR access. Erick manages most NR functionality at BOCO and is the point of contact rather than IT.

## Why separate

DEC-009 establishes the principle: tier-1 credentials are owned by `claude-session`, not borrowed from hactar. The ingest key needs to be on a separate principal so that:
- Telemetry from sandboxed sessions is attributable to `claude-session` in NR's account view (rather than mixed with hactar's interactive Claude usage)
- Rotation of hactar's NR access doesn't disrupt agent sessions, and vice-versa
- Damage-radius if leaked is bounded to "fake telemetry submission under claude-session principal"

## Acceptance

- NR license key issued, scoped to ingest only (no read access, no admin actions)
- Key stored under `/home/claude-session/.config/<NR config dir>/...` with mode 600 owned by `claude-session`
- Sandboxed `claude` session emits OTEL traces successfully (no `FailedToOpenSocket` / `ECONNREFUSED` in claude --debug-to-stderr output)
- DEC-010 records the credential per the DEC-008 tier-1 requirement

## Open

- Confirm with Erick whether NR's API supports a "service-account"-style principal or whether the key is just user-bound. If the latter, this is the escape-hatch case (personal token transferred to claude-session) and we need DEC-NNN (escape-hatch) referenced.
notes: Note 2026-05-05: the original description anticipated 'DEC-010 records the credential' — DEC-010 was filed for the escape-hatch policy. The NR credential's DEC entry will be the next available number when the credential is actually provisioned (likely DEC-011).

title: Jira/Atlassian readonly token (claude-session, interim personal token)
description:
Create a personal Jira/Atlassian API token with a Claude-adjacent name (e.g., "claude-session-readonly") for auditability, and transfer ownership to `claude-session` via the escape-hatch mechanism. This is the *interim* tier-1 Jira credential pending a dedicated service account from BOCO IT (see sibling bead).

## Mechanism

Per the escape-hatch (DEC-NNN, pending): user creates token at id.atlassian.com with a clearly Claude-attributed name. Transfer to claude-session via `share-credential.sh` (option a — copy + chown + chmod 600, optionally GPG-encrypted to claude-session's pubkey).

## Scope discipline

The token inherits the user's Atlassian permissions, which is broader than ideal. Mitigation: rely on the Claude-attributed name for audit trails, document what scopes/projects Claude actually uses (BOCO project keys), and prioritize the service-account follow-up.

## Acceptance

- Token created in id.atlassian.com with a name that makes attribution obvious in Jira audit logs (e.g., "claude-session-readonly-<date>")
- Token transferred to /home/claude-session/.config/atlassian/token via share-credential.sh
- A sandboxed Claude session can read Jira tickets via the Atlassian MCP tool (or jira-cli equivalent)
- DEC-011 records the credential as a tier-1-by-escape-hatch entry per DEC-008

## Supersession

Closes when the dedicated service account (sibling bead) is provisioned.
notes: Note 2026-05-05: the original description anticipated 'DEC-011 records the credential' — that number was reserved for escape-hatch implications and may shift. The interim Jira credential's DEC entry will be the next available number when provisioned (likely DEC-012, given DEC-011 may go to NR).

title: bd-timew init-project: doesn't scaffold .beads/bd-timew.yaml sidecar
description:
`bd-timew init-project --server` registers the repo with the global cleanup config (~/.config/bd-timew/repos.yaml) but does NOT create the per-project sidecar at `.beads/bd-timew.yaml`. The user has to discover and run `bd-timew config init` separately to scaffold the sidecar.

Result: time tracking against beads in the new project produces `Client/Case/Svc: (none)` until the user notices the gap and runs `config init` manually.

Discovered 2026-05-05 starting work on ClaudeConfig-40s.4 in claude-config — the original migration ran `init-project` per the published recipe, the sidecar was never created, and the gap surfaced only when the first `bd-timew start` showed empty tuple values.

Proposed fix: `bd-timew init-project` should run `bd-timew config init` as part of project bootstrap (or warn loudly when no sidecar exists at the end of init-project). Either approach makes new-project setup correct-by-default.

Related (init-project gap family):
- J121-i85 (auto-push hang during settings bootstrap)
- J121-xji (bootstrap bd init when .beads/ is missing)

title: Reviewer dispatch
description: Coordinator spawns a claude-sandbox read-only checkout of a worker's branch. Reviewer examines git diff main..., posts review via bead comment. No filesystem access to the worker's working directory; collaboration happens through git only.

title: Bead state machine + heartbeat/timeout pattern
description: State machine: open → claimed → in_progress → ready_for_review → reviewed → ready_to_merge → merged. Heartbeat from worker prevents orphaned in_progress beads. Timeout escalation surfaces stuck workers.

title: Coordinator/human merge process documentation
description: Document the bd merge workflow: how the coordinator (or human) reviews an agent's claude/* branch and merges to main. Include the non-fast-forward case where main has moved during the session.

title: Coordinator process design + skeleton
description: Long-lived systemd-managed coordinator. Reads bd ready --label=area:agent-tasks, dispatches workers, observes their progress, dispatches reviewers, surfaces ready-to-merge work. Single-file design doc + initial skeleton.

title: Worker dispatch
description: Coordinator spawns a claude-sandbox session in a fresh worktree + Dolt branch, scoped to one bead. Profile selection per task class. Worker runs, commits, pushes branch, updates bead status.

title: Branch-pattern GRANTs for bd-claude-session
description: GRANT SELECT on all branches; GRANT INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE only on branches matching claude/*. Verify with negative test: an attempt to write to main fails with auth denied.

title: Per-session claude/<session-id> Dolt branch lifecycle
description: Wrapper creates a Dolt branch matching the git session-id at sandbox startup; deletes (or merges) at session end. BEADS_DOLT_USER and BEADS_DOLT_PASSWORD for bd-claude-session set inside the namespace; user's own bd-write credentials not exposed.

title: bd-claude-session Dolt user creation script
description: SQL bootstrap script that creates the bd-claude-session Dolt user with a generated password (stored in pass). Idempotent: re-runs do not error if the user exists.

title: memory/agent-observations/ subdirectory convention
description: Auto-memory MEMORY.md does not auto-load this subtree. Agent observations land here; human reviews and promotes selected entries to the main memory tree. Treat agent memory like agent code — propose, review, promote.

title: Verify Phase 1 ACLs grant write-back through bind mount
description: Verify that the ACLs set up in Phase 1 grant claude-session write access to ~/.claude/projects/<hash>/ when accessed through the namespace bind mount. bd memories writes from inside the sandbox must reach the canonical location.

title: Concurrent-session smoke test
description: Verify two parallel claude-sandbox invocations are independent: each in its own worktree, neither sees the other's filesystem; pushed branches visible only via git fetch. Documented procedure in docs/SANDBOX_GUIDE.md.

title: Bind-mount worktree at canonical path inside namespace
description: Inside the agent's mount namespace, bind-mount the worktree at the canonical project path (/home/hactar/Source/<project>/). Same path string the user's interactive session uses, so the project-hash for ~/.claude/projects/<hash>/ matches. See Common Pitfall #2.

title: Reference profiles: default + full-trust
description: Ship reference profiles in this repo: default.yaml (baseline, nothing extra exposed) and full-trust.yaml (escape hatch for emergency parity with pre-sandbox behavior). Documentation for when to use full-trust.

title: Session ID generation + tracking
description: Session IDs (timestamp + short random suffix). Wrapper records active sessions in a state file or via systemd-run scope. Used by Phase 5 for claude/<session-id> Dolt branches.

title: Auto-worktree creation + teardown
description: git worktree add -b session-<id> <path> origin/main at session start; git worktree remove at session end. Idempotent.

title: Profile YAML schema design + documentation
description: Document the profile schema in sandbox/profiles/SCHEMA.md. Keys: visible filesystem paths (read-only / read-write), worktree policy (auto-create / use-existing / disabled), skill subset, network policy. Schema enforcement: profiles MUST NOT contain credentials: or env: keys for secret injection (DEC-001). Loader rejects offending profiles.

title: Profile loader implementation
description: Wrapper reads ~/.config/claude-sandbox/profiles/<active>.yaml at session start; consumed before claude exec. Schema validation at load time.

title: Profile discovery: --profile flag + per-project .claude/sandbox-profile
description: Two discovery mechanisms: (a) --profile <name> on the wrapper, (b) per-project .claude/sandbox-profile file naming the active profile. Default to 'default' if neither present.

title: Phase 1 manual smoke test documentation
description: Document the manual smoke test (claude-sandbox --version; claude-sandbox -p 'echo hello'; verify credentials empty; verify ~/.claude bind-mount). Lives in docs/SANDBOX_GUIDE.md. Manual rather than CI-driven because CI cannot easily execute the privileged namespace setup.

title: One-time OAuth flow for claude-session
description: claude-session does its own one-time browser OAuth flow with the user's company subscription. Credentials stored in /home/claude-session/.claude/.credentials.json. Document the bootstrap experience: first claude-sandbox invocation lands in a session with no auth; the user completes the browser flow inside that sandboxed session.

title: Phase 6: autosession daemon
description:
Long-lived coordinator agent that dispatches short-lived worker and reviewer agents against a beads task queue. Single human-in-the-loop checkpoint at merge. See docs/ROADMAP.md#phase-6.

Related: J121-9kp.2.17 family (auto-session skill, deferred) — a session-launcher informs the daemon design but is not the daemon itself.

Acceptance: coordinator runs unattended overnight; workers and reviewers dispatched correctly; ready-to-merge work surfaces for human in morning.

title: Phase 5: Dolt user + branch-restricted beads
description:
Sandboxed agents read all bead state, write to claude/<session-id> branch only. Bead state mutation flows through code-review-style merge workflow. See DEC-002 and docs/ROADMAP.md#phase-5.

Acceptance: bd-claude-session can SELECT all branches; INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on claude/* only; attempts to write main fail with auth denied; merge workflow surfaces agent's branch for review.

title: Phase 3: per-session namespace + worktree creation
description:
Parallel sessions in independent namespaces, each with its own session-specific feature-branch worktree. Agents collaborate via git/beads, never via shared filesystems. See docs/ROADMAP.md#phase-3.

Acceptance: two parallel claude-sandbox sessions, each in its own worktree, neither sees the other's filesystem; pushed branches visible only via git fetch.

title: Phase 4: path-aliasing for memory continuity
description:
Auto-memory + claude-mem observations work correctly inside sandboxed sessions. Worktree bind-mounted at canonical project path inside namespace so project-hash matches. See docs/ROADMAP.md#phase-4.

Acceptance: bd memories inside sandbox returns same memories as user's interactive session for the same project. claude-mem observations recorded by agent appear in canonical corpus.

title: bd init: warn when backup.git-push will conflict with existing git remote
description:
# Context

Upstream enhancement for bd (not bd-timew). `bd init --server` enables
JSONL backup auto-push by default whenever the target repo has a git
remote configured. The backup pushes to `refs/heads/main`, which
conflicts with normal git activity on the same branch and produces a
multi-minute hang on every subsequent bd write.

Detailed analysis of the conflict pattern is in J121-i85 (the bd-timew
side). This bead is for the upstream bd enhancement: bd init itself
could warn or guard against this configuration at init time.

# Proposed enhancement

At `bd init` time, detect whether all of these conditions hold:

- `bd init --server` is being run (or backup features will be auto-
  enabled).
- The target repo has an existing git remote (i.e., `git remote get-url
  origin` succeeds).
- `backup.git-push` defaults to enabled in this codepath.

If all three: emit a warning at the end of `bd init` with the conflict
explanation and the recommended fix:

```
WARNING: JSONL backup auto-push is enabled by default and will push to
refs/heads/main on the configured git remote. If this remote is the
same as your source-code repo, the backup pushes will conflict with
your normal commits and produce hangs on every bd write.

To disable JSONL backup auto-push:
    bd config set backup.git-push false

Or to disable backup entirely:
    bd config set backup.enabled false

This warning can be suppressed via --suppress-backup-warning.
```

Alternative (more aggressive): default `backup.git-push` to false when
the repo has a git remote that's the same URL bd's dolt remote will use.
The current default (auto-enable based on git-remote presence) is a
footgun in the common case where the user is initializing beads in their
existing source repo.

# Out of scope for this bead

The bd-timew side of the same problem is J121-i85. That's a separate fix
in a separate codebase and proceeds independently.

# Discovery context

Found 2026-05-04 during initial setup of the claude-config project's
beads instance. See J121-i85 for full root-cause analysis. Filing as
priority 3 because the bd-timew fix in J121-i85 will mitigate the most
common discovery path; this enhancement is for users who hit the issue
while using bd directly (not via bd-timew init-project).

title: bd-timew init-project: bootstrap bd init when .beads/ is missing
description:
# Symptom

`bd-timew init-project` exits with the error:

```
bd-timew: no active Beads workspace. cd into a project with .beads/, or
pass --project-dir.
```

when run in a project that does not yet have `.beads/` initialized. This
is the exact use case the command name suggests it handles — the user
expectation for `init-project` is "initialize a project for bd-timew use,"
which implicitly includes running `bd init` if needed.

The command currently only configures bd-timew settings on top of an
already-existing `.beads/` directory. The required prerequisite (`bd
init`) is undocumented, and the command name is misleading.

# Root cause

`cmd_init_project` in `src/bd_timew/project.py` opens with:

```python
def cmd_init_project(...):
    beads_dir = find_beads_dir(path)   # line 232
    ...
```

`find_beads_dir` (in `src/bd_timew/util.py:92`) requires `.beads/` to
exist; it exits with the "no active Beads workspace" error otherwise.

# Proposed fix

Detect missing `.beads/` and run `bd init` first, forwarding relevant
flags. Patch at the top of `cmd_init_project`:

```python
def cmd_init_project(...):
    candidate_root = (path or Path.cwd()).resolve()
    if not (candidate_root / ".beads").is_dir():
        root_log.info("No .beads/ at %s; bootstrapping with 'bd init'", candidate_root)
        bd_init_cmd = ["bd", "init"]
        if server_mode:
            bd_init_cmd.append("--server")
        if not is_interactive() or yes:
            bd_init_cmd.append("--non-interactive")
        run(bd_init_cmd, cwd=candidate_root, check=True)

    beads_dir = find_beads_dir(path)
    # ... rest unchanged
```

Three details worth getting right:

1. **Server-mode forwarding.** If `--server` was passed to bd-timew,
   forward it to `bd init`. If `--no-server`, don't pass `--server`. If
   the user didn't specify (`server_mode is None`), either move the
   server-mode prompt above the bootstrap so the answer is known, or
   default to no-server for the bootstrap and let the user re-run if
   they later answer yes.
2. **Non-interactive forwarding.** When `--yes` is passed to bd-timew or
   stdin isn't a tty, forward `--non-interactive` to `bd init` so it
   doesn't hang on prompts.
3. **Interaction with J121-i85.** Once that bead's fix lands (writing
   `backup.git-push: false` to `.beads/config.yaml` before any bd config
   sets), this bootstrap should be sequenced AFTER the bd init call but
   BEFORE the settings loop — so that the backup-disable write lands
   into the just-created `.beads/config.yaml`.

# Test reproduction

```bash
mkdir /tmp/bdtest && cd /tmp/bdtest
git init
bd-timew init-project --server
# observe: bd-timew: no active Beads workspace. cd into a project with
# .beads/, or pass --project-dir.
```

Expected behavior: `init-project` runs `bd init --server` itself, then
proceeds with the bd-timew configuration.

# Discovery context

Found 2026-05-04 during initial setup of the claude-config project's
beads instance. The user's expectation was that `bd-timew init-project`
would handle the full init flow; finding it didn't was a friction point
that required a separate bug filing. Pairs with J121-i85 (the auto-push
hang during the same setup flow).

title: bd-timew servers: surface unregistered running dolt servers
description:
bd-timew servers (cmd_servers in server.py:53-85) iterates only ~/.config/bd-timew/repos.yaml. Projects whose Dolt servers were started by bd init --server (not bd-timew init-project) are silently absent from the listing — they were never registered with bd-timew. Discovered 2026-05-04 with a chezmoi beads instance: created via bd init --server, running, but not shown.

This fails as soon as you have multiple beads workspaces, which is the use case --server mode is for.

## Proposed fix

Make cmd_servers do two passes:

1. List registered repos from repos.yaml as today (canonical source — provides last-activity, idle time, configured server settings).
2. Also scan running dolt sql-server processes (pgrep -f 'dolt sql-server', resolve /proc/<pid>/cwd to get the workspace), and report any whose project root isn't in repos.yaml as '[unregistered] <path>'. Include a hint pointing at 'bd-timew init-project --path <path>' so the user can promote them to first-class entries.

This preserves the rich treatment for registered repos while ensuring unregistered servers don't go missing in the dark.

## Scope
- bd_timew/server.py: cmd_servers (extend with second pass)
- Tests: stub /proc/<pid>/cwd reads or mock the discovery helper
- README: mention the unregistered-detection behavior

## Bundling
File alongside the other bug+enhancement beads being prepared by another session (2026-05-04). Likely folded into v0.2.0 before tag, since v0.2.0 is the queue-refactor release and this is the same surface area.

title: bd-timew init-project: auto-push hang during settings bootstrap
description:
# Symptom

`bd-timew init-project --server` (run from a freshly `bd init --server`-ed
project that has a configured git remote) hangs indefinitely on the first
`bd config set` invocation. After ~10 minutes per attempt, the dolt-server
log shows the underlying push being rejected:

```
To git+ssh://git@github.com/AudensAurantius/<project>
 ! [rejected]            main -> main (non-fast-forward)
```

Reproduction: tested in two repos (claude-config and chezmoi). Same hang
in both.

# Root cause

bd's JSONL backup auto-push is enabled by default whenever the target
repo has a git remote configured. The backup pushes JSONL to
`refs/heads/main` via regular `git push`, which conflicts with the
project's normal git activity on the same branch.

This is **independent** of two other push mechanisms easy to confuse it
with:

1. `no-push` (bd config) — gates bd's own dolt-push after writes. Does
   NOT control the JSONL backup.
2. `dolt_replicate_to_remote` (dolt persisted variable) — server-side
   replication. OFF by default in `bd init --server` (verified via
   `SELECT @@dolt_replicate_to_remote`).
3. `backup.git-push` (bd config) — JSONL backup git push. **ON by
   default when a git remote exists.** ← the actual culprit.

The dolt remote configured by `bd init --server` is fine: it uses the
`refs/dolt/data` namespace and doesn't conflict with regular git
branches. Only the JSONL backup uses `refs/heads/*`.

`bd-timew init-project` triggers the hang because every `bd config set`
goes through the dolt server, which triggers a write commit, which
triggers the JSONL backup push, which conflicts and hangs. The very
setting that would help (`no-push: true`) doesn't actually help because
the JSONL backup is a separate mechanism.

# Proposed fix

Before applying any `bd config set` calls, write `backup.git-push: false`
directly to `.beads/config.yaml` via file I/O to break the chicken-and-
egg. Suggested location: `cmd_init_project` in `src/bd_timew/project.py`,
after `find_beads_dir(path)` (line 232) and before the settings loop
(line 303):

```python
config_yaml = beads_dir / "config.yaml"
content = config_yaml.read_text()
if "backup:" not in content:
    content += "\nbackup:\n  git-push: false\n"
    config_yaml.write_text(content)
    root_log.info("Disabled JSONL backup auto-push (avoids init-project hang)")
```

After this writes the YAML directly, the existing `bd config set` loop
runs without triggering the hang.

Add an opt-in flag (`--enable-backup-push`) for users whose target
configuration genuinely wants the JSONL backup enabled (rare; mostly
applies to dedicated beads-only repos where there's no source code on
`refs/heads/main` to conflict with).

# Test reproduction

```bash
mkdir /tmp/bdtest && cd /tmp/bdtest
git init && git remote add origin git@github.com:user/some-repo.git
bd init --server
bd config set no-push true   # observe hang (~10 min)
# Inspect .beads/dolt-server.log for `main -> main (non-fast-forward)`
```

# User-facing workaround until fixed

Immediately after `bd init --server`, before any other bd writes:

```bash
cat >> .beads/config.yaml <<'YAML'

backup:
  git-push: false
YAML
```

Then `bd-timew init-project --server` proceeds normally.

# Discovery context

Found 2026-05-04 during initial setup of the claude-config project's
beads instance. See claude-config repo:
docs/migration/beads-init-plan.md and
claude/skills/beads-migration/SKILL.md for the workflow context that
surfaced this.
notes:
## Correction (2026-05-04, after further diagnosis)

Initial root-cause analysis was wrong on two counts:

1. **Dolt server-side replication is NOT enabled by default.** Verified via
   `SELECT @@dolt_replicate_to_remote, @@dolt_replicate_all_heads,
   @@dolt_replicate_heads, @@dolt_async_replication` against a fresh
   `bd init --server` instance — all empty/0. The hang is not from dolt's
   own replication.

2. **The hang is from bd's JSONL backup auto-push, not dolt's remote.**
   bd init --server enables `backup.git-push` by default whenever the
   target repo has a git remote configured. The backup pushes JSONL to
   `refs/heads/main` via regular `git push`, which conflicts with the
   project's normal git activity on the same branch.

The dolt-server log error `main -> main (non-fast-forward)` is from this
regular git push, not from dolt-internal replication.

## Three independent push mechanisms

Conflated in the original bead:

1. **`no-push`** (bd config) — gates bd's own `dolt push` invocations
   after writes. Was applied during the hung init-project (visible in
   `.beads/config.yaml`), but doesn't help.
2. **`dolt_replicate_to_remote`** (dolt persisted variable) — server-side
   replication; OFF by default.
3. **`backup.git-push`** (bd config) — JSONL backup; **ON by default when
   a git remote exists**. ← The actual culprit.

## Revised fix shape for bd-timew init-project

Before applying any bd config writes, set `backup.git-push: false` (or
`backup.enabled: false`) directly to `.beads/config.yaml` via file write
to break the chicken-and-egg. The current code path does
`bd config set` for each setting in order, but each invocation goes
through the dolt server which triggers the JSONL backup push, which hangs.

Suggested implementation: write a YAML stanza directly to
`.beads/config.yaml` via Python BEFORE any subprocess invocation of `bd
config set`:

```python
# In project.py, before line 303 (the settings loop):
config_yaml = beads_dir / "config.yaml"
content = config_yaml.read_text()
if "backup:" not in content:
    content += "\nbackup:\n  git-push: false\n"
    config_yaml.write_text(content)
```

Then proceed with `bd config set` calls as before. The first one no longer
triggers the JSONL backup push because backup is now disabled at
file-config level.

Add a `--enable-backup-push` opt-in flag for users whose target setup
genuinely wants the JSONL backup to push (rare; mostly applies to
dedicated beads repos like the J121-beads pattern).

## Re-scoping

This is a single bug with one fix point in bd-timew init-project. The
"two bugs" framing in the original description (bd init misconfig +
bd-timew sequencing) was wrong — there's no bd init bug. The dolt remote
configured by bd init --server uses refs/dolt/data namespace and doesn't
conflict with normal git activity. Only the JSONL backup conflicts.

bd init *could* still be improved by warning when the target repo has an
existing git remote AND backup.git-push will default to enabled. That's a
separate bd upstream enhancement, not bd-timew's problem.

title: Research: dolt sql-server + branch-pinning for auto-session sandboxing (Tier 2)
description:
Investigation bead: establish whether `dolt sql-server` with branch-pinning + scoped SQL grants can provide a Tier 2 beads sandbox — stronger than Tier 1 (permission-deny) without the duplicated-storage cost of Tier 3 (physical clone).

## Context

Discovered during the 2026-04-22 auto-session design conversation (sibling beads: Tier 1 active, Tier 3 deferred). The Tier 2 idea:

- Launch `dolt sql-server -B autosession/<slug> --port 3307` on the `.beads/` Dolt DB — server pinned to the autosession branch.
- Create a SQL user (`sandboxed`) with `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` on bd's data tables, but no grants on `dolt_branches`, `dolt_remotes`, or `DOLT_*()` system functions.
- Point bd at the server via whatever connection config bd supports, rather than embedded `.dolt/`.
- Coordinator has full bead mutation access but mechanically cannot switch branches or touch remote config.

The hinge question: **can bd flip from embedded-Dolt mode to remote-server mode cleanly?**

## Unknowns to resolve

1. **Backend flip.** Does bd expose connection config for a Dolt sql-server (host/port/user/password)? If not, is the patch to add it small (bd's Dolt layer is reportedly thin) or structural?
2. **System-function containment.** Do `DOLT_*()` functions respect SQL user grants? Specifically: can a user with `SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` but no `EXECUTE` on `DOLT_CHECKOUT()` still query data from the pinned branch? Need to test — Dolt's system-function privilege model isn't identical to vanilla MySQL's.
3. **Branch pinning mechanics.** What exactly does `-B <branch>` do on `dolt sql-server` — lock the server to that branch, or just default new connections? Can a SQL user with `USE` privilege still switch branches via `CALL DOLT_CHECKOUT(...)` even on a pinned server?
4. **bd sync interaction.** bd's `issues.jsonl` export assumes embedded DB access. Does the export pathway still work against a sql-server backend, or would it need to be bypassed during sandbox sessions?
5. **Storage/startup cost.** sql-server per autosession is a daemon process; evaluate whether the startup latency and resource overhead make Tier 2 worth it compared to Tier 1's zero-process-overhead or Tier 3's duplicated-storage cost.

## Proposed probe

One-shot exploratory session — no production wiring:

1. Stand up a `dolt sql-server` on `.beads/` (throwaway scratch clone first; do not point at the canonical).
2. Create a scoped SQL user with the hypothesised grant set.
3. Attempt common bd operations against the server (manual SQL; bd-CLI flip may not be available yet).
4. Attempt branch-mutating operations as the scoped user to confirm they fail.
5. Document findings in a review doc: which unknowns are now resolved, which are still open, and whether Tier 2 is viable at all.

## Outputs

- `docs/reviews/dolt-sql-server-autosession-sandbox-<date>.md` — findings doc with yes/no/qualified verdict on Tier 2 viability.
- Updates to this bead: promote to implementation task if Tier 2 is viable; close as `--reason="not viable"` otherwise, with the Tier 3 sibling as the fallback.

## Why separate from the Tier 1 and Tier 3 beads

Tier 2 is a research question with a binary outcome — not an implementation task. Filing it as a peer bead (rather than a TODO inside Tier 1) prevents it from getting lost, gives it its own graph edges (`discovered-from` J121-9kp.2.17.1, `relates-to` the two sibling tiers), and makes the "I looked, here's what I found" output a first-class deliverable.

## Estimate

~2-4 hours for the probe + findings doc. Implementation work (if Tier 2 is viable) would be a separate bead filed off this one's findings.
notes: FINDINGS (2026-04-27): Tier 2 as conceived is not feasible. (1) dolt sql-server -B flag does not exist. (2) Two servers cannot share data dir — exclusive write lock. (3) Session-level branch isolation DOES work (USE `db/branch` and DOLT_CHECKOUT work per-connection) but bd's database name validator rejects slashes in BEADS_DOLT_SERVER_DATABASE, blocking the connection path. (4) Tier 2 requires upstream bd feature: --db-branch or startup-SQL option. Tier 1 (permission deny) is ready to ship. Full findings: tasks/J121-9kp-2-17-5/dolt-sandbox-tier2-findings.md.
close_reason: Research complete. Tier 2 as conceived (separate branch-pinned server) is not feasible: -B flag doesn't exist, exclusive data-dir lock prevents second server. Session-level branch isolation works in raw SQL but bd's validator rejects db/branch format in BEADS_DOLT_SERVER_DATABASE. Requires upstream bd startup-SQL feature. Recommendations: ship Tier 1 now, file upstream feature request, defer Tier 2. Full findings + 3 bd memories recorded.

title: beads-reconcile: materialize inherited billing labels + bd-timew child-label propagation
description:
Hybrid design (C+D+E) to make Beads labels the single source of truth for billing-tuple resolution across the full dependency graph, without requiring every descendant to be manually labeled.

SCOPE
1. (D) Write `beads-reconcile` script that walks parent-child edges and materializes inherited label dimensions from ancestors onto descendants.
2. (C) Wrap bd-timew (or the child-create path) to forward-propagate inheritable labels at create time — so new children inherit without waiting for reconcile to run.
3. (E) Convention: epics declare the canonical billing tuple (svc:, case:, client:) once; descendants resolve via materialization. No need to touch every sub-bead manually.

ACCEPTANCE
- `.beads/bd-timew.yaml` grows an `inherit:` key listing inheritable dimensions (e.g., `[svc:, case:, client:]`).
- Running `beads-reconcile` on current workspace state produces zero changes (because 2026-04-21 manual pass already applied svc:/case: to all 49 src:jira-closure beads).
- Creating a new child under a labeled parent (via bd-timew wrapper or documented flow) auto-inherits the listed dimensions.
- `bd list --label 'svc:Technology Services'` returns the full closure with no client-side graph walk needed.
- `/next --src jira` and friends work without post-filtering for descendants.
- Documented integration point with `bd jira sync --pull` so newly-imported tickets get reconciled.

SEE: design field for full decision context, alternatives considered, and open questions for design resumption.
design:
DESIGN DISCUSSION (2026-04-21, to resume when claimed)

## Problem
Jira-sourced beads carry src:jira + billing dimensions (svc:, case:). Their descendants (type:derived sub-beads Claude or users file during work) carry neither automatically. bd-timew reads the active bead's labels to resolve a (client, case, svc) Timewarrior tuple via pattern match in .beads/bd-timew.yaml. Descendants without labels fall through to defaults, silently mis-billing.

## Alternatives considered

| Option | Resolves /next filter | Resolves bd-timew tuple | Stale-descendants risk | Ergonomic cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A. Post-filter closure in /next | yes | no (bd-timew reads per-bead labels, not through /next) | none | only solves /next |
| B. billable:true/false label on every bead | yes | partial | forgot-to-set on new bead | high: every author remembers |
| C. bd-timew wrapper creates children with inherited labels | yes | yes | only if wrapper bypassed | medium: forward-only, existing beads not fixed |
| D. beads-reconcile script: walk graph, materialize inherited labels on descendants | yes | yes | only between runs, idempotent | low: cron/pre-commit |
| E. Epic-owns-tuple only, descendants resolve via ancestor lookup | NO (bd --label is exact-match) | NO (bd-timew reads labels, doesn't walk graph) | always | can't work standalone |

## Decision
Hybrid C + D with E for organizational transparency. E pairs with D: epics declare the tuple once (humans see the logical ownership), D materializes it down (machines filter natively). C is the forward-propagation convenience so you don't need to run reconcile after every bead create.

## Open questions for implementation
1. **Where does beads-reconcile live?** Options: (a) ~/.claude/scripts/ (global, chezmoi-managed), (b) .beads/scripts/ in the per-project .beads repo (portable with the workspace), (c) tasks/beads-reconcile/scripts/ in the task dir. Preference: (b) — travels with the workspace, rides the existing AudensAurantius/J121-beads remote.
2. **Conflict resolution.** If a descendant already has a different value for an inherited dimension (e.g., parent svc:Technology Services, child svc:none for a scope:local personal sub-task), which wins? Proposal: child wins (explicit label beats inherited); reconcile only fills missing dimensions, never overwrites. Need to decide if scope:local should be a hard exclusion (skip inheritance entirely).
3. **Label removal semantics.** If a label is removed from an ancestor, should reconcile strip it from descendants? Risky. Proposal: inheritance is additive-only; removing from descendants is manual.
4. **Edge types.** Only parent-child counts for inheritance (simple). blocks/depends_on edges do not propagate labels.
5. **Sync integration.** bd jira sync --pull imports new Jira tickets. Reconcile should run after. Options: (a) post-sync hook in bd config, (b) wrap sync in a bd-jira-reconcile script, (c) manual discipline. Preference: (a) if bd supports post-hook; else (b).
6. **Pre-commit hook or scheduled.** Running on every commit to .beads is cheap (tens of ms on ~140 beads). Scheduled/manual is simpler. Preference: pre-commit hook in .beads/, scheduled every N hours as backstop.
7. **Epic declaration convention.** Where do epics live today — do all top-level Jira-synced beads count as epics, or only type=epic? BOCO-18077 (J121-n39) is a task that became a parent; it's effectively an epic by graph position. Proposal: inheritance triggers from any bead that carries inheritable labels, regardless of type.

## Prerequisites / context
- 2026-04-21 manual pass already applied svc:'Technology Services' + case:'07_04 Portal Stabilization Phase 1.1 (Project Task)' to 49 beads (closure of 27 open src:jira seeds + 11 closed seeds + 11 descendants). See bd remember billing-labels-on-jira-beads-all-src-jira.
- bd accepts label values with spaces, periods, parens. Verified 2026-04-21 via 'bd update --add-label test:with space'.
- bd-timew already does pattern match on labels (see .beads/bd-timew.yaml default + patterns blocks). Reconcile extends this model; bd-timew can stay unchanged if reconcile is correct.
- .beads/ is a nested git repo at AudensAurantius/J121-beads; both scripts and config travel with it.

title: bd-timew variance reporting: est-h vs actual for a bead

title: Migrate existing systemd timers under Chezmoi management
description:
Infra cleanup: existing bd-timew safety-net systemd user timers (see memory/checkpoint-2026-04-19-timew-workflow-and-agents.md) should be moved under Chezmoi control so they are machine-portable and committed.

Steps:

title: systematic-debugging cherry-pick from Superpowers
description:
Cherry-pick the systematic-debugging skill from Superpowers (obra/superpowers) as a standalone global skill. Do NOT install the whole Superpowers plugin (per research file tasks/J121-dbo/research/04-superpowers-gsd.md — methodology lock-in conflicts with existing Beads/claude-mem/auto-memory stack).

Skill content: structured debugging workflow — state hypothesis before testing, design minimal test to distinguish hypotheses, observe, update mental model, iterate. Prevents the failure mode of jumping to conclusions before exhausting hypothesis space.

Implementation: locate the skill markdown in the Superpowers repo, adapt/port into ~/.claude/skills/ with any wording tweaks needed to fit the user's style. Delivered via Chezmoi per standing conventions.

Also in scope: evaluate while porting whether verification-before-completion has a concrete checklist worth porting into CLAUDE.md (separate tiny bead handles that; this bead only delivers systematic-debugging as a standalone skill).

title: Python scripting skill
description:
Global skill that Claude consults when writing Python scripts. Codifies user-specific conventions that are not Claude defaults.

Conventions enforced:
- logging module instead of bare print
- argparse parser construction in a dedicated get_cli_arguments() function for single-file scripts
- ArgumentParser uses custom HelpFormatter inheriting from argparse.RawDescriptionHelpFormatter + argparse.ArgumentDefaultsHelpFormatter
- Parser constructor sets an epilog where appropriate (see snowflake_migrations/scripts/get_snowflake_procs.py for reference)
- Credentials read from config file or environment variables; never declared as constants
- Machine-specific details via pathlib / existing config files / CLI args / env vars (never hardcoded)
- Library-native config APIs preferred (e.g., snowflake library for snow CLI config, not hand-rolled TOML parser)
- ruff formatter + linter applied
- Purpose-built libraries preferred over regex text manipulation (example: sqlglot for SQL parsing/construction)
- Scripts developed and tested in venvs unless explicitly system-wide
- --loglevel CLI arg always exposed for verbosity control
- Justfile recipe added if Justfile exists in the project
- Credentials exported via .envrc from pass store (confirm on skill execution)

Scope: global. Backed by J121-91l reference doc; skill consumes the reference, not replaces it. Delivered via Chezmoi per standing conventions.

title: Jira skill + Jira agent + /draft-comment + /post-comment
description:
Global Jira integration bundle. Four deliverables in one bead because they share the ADF/mention/frontmatter conventions:

Jira conventions skill (global, doc-only) — codifies ADF patterns, mention resolution (@{Display Name} → ADF mention node), draft frontmatter (posted, comment_id, target_status, contentFormat: adf), comment-style guidance (lead with context/methodology, integrate links, connect to team knowledge, no prescriptive next-steps in analysis comments). 2. Jira agent (global subagent) — takes a ticket context + guidance, produces an ADF draft. Isolates draft-writing context from the main conversation; ticket-history reads go to the subagent. Agent system prompt references the Jira conventions skill.

3. /draft-comment <jira-id> [guidance] — invokes the Jira agent, drops result as comments/YYYY-MM-DD-<jira-id>.md in the current task directory with proper frontmatter (posted: false, contentFormat: adf, target_status, jira_id).

4. /post-comment <draft-path> — resolves @{mentions} to ADF mention nodes, posts via addCommentToJiraIssue MCP tool, updates frontmatter (posted: true, comment_id, posted_at).

Scope: global. All four delivered via Chezmoi per standing conventions. Jira conventions span BOCO projects, not J121-only.

title: Design Phase 7 executor as multi-backend dispatcher (E2B / AIO / K8s candidates)
description:
Surfaced by ClaudeConfig-40s.12 (sandbox-tooling survey, 2026-05-05). Design the Phase 7 privileged-action executor as a multi-backend dispatcher rather than building sandbox-spawning ourselves for capabilities that just need "execute untrusted code."

## The reframing

Phase 7 (per DEC-003) was originally framed as "build a mediator daemon that *executes* privileged actions." The survey suggests an alternative framing: **build the mediator as a *dispatcher* with pluggable execution backends.**

Specific backend candidates per capability class:

- **Untrusted code execution** (Jupyter, build scripts, data transforms): dispatch to E2B (or self-hosted Firecracker analog). Apache-2.0 OSS infra exists; SDKs in Python/TS.
- **Browser / GUI / computer-use automation**: dispatch to agent-infra/sandbox (AIO Sandbox). Bundled Chrome+VNC+code-server is fit-for-purpose. Note: weaker isolation (Docker + relaxed seccomp); only suitable for capabilities where the backend's threat surface is acceptable.
- **K8s pod scheduling** (only if user runs a cluster — not our user, but relevant for shareable-tool future scope): dispatch to a kubernetes-sigs/agent-sandbox `SandboxClaim`.

## Why this matters

Reduces Phase 7 implementation surface meaningfully. Writing a hardened-runtime spawner is multi-week work; integrating with E2B's SDK is days. The mediator daemon, capability manifest, audit log, and request schema are still our work — those are the high-trust components — but the actual sandbox-spawning becomes a backend driver per capability class.

Also: when Phase 7 design begins, DEC-003 will be replaced with a non-provisional decision entry. The dispatcher framing should land in that revision.

## Acceptance

- DEC-003's replacement (filed at Phase 7 implementation start) records the dispatcher design with named candidate backends.
- A capability manifest schema is defined that includes a `backend:` field per capability.
- At least one backend driver is implemented (E2B is the lightest first target).
- Survey artifacts referenced: .tasks/40s.12-sandbox-tooling-survey/SUMMARY.md.

## Open

- Self-hosted Firecracker (without E2B SaaS) feasibility for single-workstation deployment — worth a prototype scan when Phase 7 begins.
- BOCO's procurement / IT stance on E2B SaaS as a developer-tool dependency.

title: Jira/Atlassian service account for claude-session (with IT)
description:
Pursue a dedicated Atlassian service account from BOCO IT for `claude-session` to use, replacing the interim personal-token arrangement (sibling bead).

## Why this matters

A personal token (interim arrangement) inherits the issuing user's full Jira permissions. A service account can be:
- Scoped to specific Jira projects (BOCO etc.) via group membership
- Read-only at the principal level, not just per-token
- Audited as a non-human entity at the IT level

This addresses the Q&A point that "Atlassian's API token model doesn't natively scope to specific projects (it's user-bound, all-or-nothing within that user's permissions). The way to get scope discipline is the user the token represents."

## Acceptance

- IT-issued service account, e.g., `claude-session@boldorange.com` or similar
- Group membership scoped to specific Jira projects (BOCO + others Claude works in)
- API token issued to that service account, transferred to claude-session via the standard tier-1 provisioning path (no escape hatch needed; the principal is already separate)
- DEC-011 amended (or DEC-012 added) to reflect the service-account version superseding the interim personal-token arrangement
- Interim Jira bead closed

## Open

- BOCO IT's policy on service accounts for AI tooling. Worth a documentation/policy ask alongside the request — sets precedent for future agent-tooling credentials.

title: Evaluate thrum PreCompact/PostCompact context-snapshot mechanism vs. claude-protocol CLAUDE.md flush
description:
## Background

The 2026-04-28 thrum assessment (tasks/thrum-assessment/thrum-assessment.md) identified thrum's PreCompact/PostCompact hook pattern as independently valuable, regardless of whether thrum itself is adopted. This bead evaluates that pattern against the approach already planned in J121-9kp.2.17.2.2 and decides which to implement (or whether to composite them).

## Two approaches in play

### Approach A — claude-protocol cherry-pick (J121-9kp.2.17.2.2 plan)
- **PreCompact hook:** shell script reminds orchestrator to flush current-state narrative to `cwd-CLAUDE.md`'s `## Current State` section before compaction fires.
- **Restore path:** existing SessionStart:compact hook reads that section on session resume.
- **Storage:** local file, same repo, no separate persistence layer.

### Approach B — thrum context-snapshot mechanism
- **PreCompact hook:** structured snapshot written as a JSONL event to the git-backed `a-sync` orphan branch. Fields include: current intent, active bead IDs, open diffs, decision log.
- **PostCompact hook:** reads the latest snapshot from git on resume; injects it as structured context rather than raw CLAUDE.md text.
- **Key difference:** snapshots are versioned, peer-addressable, and survive worktree deletion. Accessible from sibling sessions without shared filesystem.
- **Source:** thrum's `claude-plugin/SKILL.md`, `scripts/precompact.sh`, `scripts/postcompact.sh` — assess full implementation from the cloned repo at tasks/thrum-assessment/thrum/.

## Questions to resolve

1. **Coverage delta:** does thrum's structured snapshot capture context that the CLAUDE.md flush misses (e.g., staging area state, inter-session message queue, bead mutation log)?
2. **Composability:** can both run (Approach A as a human-readable fallback, Approach B as a structured machine-readable snapshot), or do they conflict?
3. **Dependency footprint:** Approach B requires thrum daemon to be running; is that acceptable for auto-session, or does it add a hard dep on a v0.9.x single-contributor binary?
4. **Bd integration:** thrum snapshots carry no Beads/Timewarrior context. Does the structured format have an extension point for injecting billing context, or does it need patching?

## Deliverable

A decision note appended to tasks/thrum-assessment/thrum-assessment.md (or a separate `precompact-decision.md` in the same dir) covering:
- Side-by-side comparison on: coverage, reliability, dependency cost, maintenance burden
- Decision: implement A, implement B, or composite
- If composite: specify the boundary (what each hook writes, which wins on restore conflict)
- Update J121-9kp.2.17.2.2's description with the outcome before that bead is claimed

Estimate: ~1.5hr (read thrum source, run both hooks mentally against a simulated compaction, write decision note).
notes:
Part of auto-session pre-ship work (J121-9kp.2.17). Blocks J121-9kp.2.17.2.2 — that bead's implementation approach depends on the decision made here. Discovered from thrum assessment 2026-04-28: tasks/thrum-assessment/thrum-assessment.md.

title: bd-tasks: include bead summary in dstask-notify toast notification
description:
Follow-on to J121-32y (bd-tasks wrapper).

Extend the dstask-notify morning toast to include a one-line Beads summary
alongside each task entry, so the notification gives full context without
requiring a terminal.

## Proposed format

Current line format:

With bead summary:
  [P1] Apply PROD Terraform plan — 10:00 AM ⚠ OVERDUE

## Implementation sketch

- dstask-notify reads the `+bead:<id>` tag from each task.
- Calls `bd show <id> --json` (or parses `bd list` output) to get the bead title.
- Appends the bead line indented below the task line.

title: bd-tasks: dstask integration wrapper — projection/execution layer under Beads
description:
Design and implement a dedicated `bd-tasks` wrapper script that makes dstask a
projection/execution layer under Beads.

## Goals

- One-way content flow: Beads is SoT for titles, due dates, and priority.
  dstask is a display/execution surface only.
- Reverse flow: status changes only (done/cancelled → bd close/update).
- Tasks in dstask are subordinate to one parent bead; they do not become
  independent Beads issues.

## Conventions

- dstask project tag: `project:<repo-name>` (e.g. `project:J121`)
- bead linkage tag: `+bead:<id>` (e.g. `+bead:J121-0cl`)
- Inherited bead labels mapped to dstask tags with namespace stripped:
  `area:pipeline` → `+pipeline`, `scope:local` → `+local`, etc.
- Due date inheritance: if a bead has a due date, `bd-tasks plan` sets it on
  the first created task by default; operator can override per task.

## Subcommands

- `bd-tasks plan <bead>` — opens \$EDITOR with a task-seed template pre-filled
  from bead title/description; on save, creates each line as a dstask task with
  correct tags and due date.
- `bd-tasks list <bead>` — lists all dstask tasks tagged `+bead:<id>`.
- `bd-tasks sync <bead>` — pushes bead title/due/priority changes back into
  linked dstask tasks (non-destructive; does not overwrite user-added notes).
- `bd-tasks complete-bead <bead>` — marks all open linked tasks done, then
  calls `bd close <bead>`.

## dstask↔timew bridge

When a task is activated in dstask (`dstask start <id>`), a companion hook or
wrapper reads the `bead:` tag and calls `bd-timew start <bead-id>` so
Timewarrior picks up the correct billing tuple without manual `/start`.

## Friction-free sub-bead spawning

If a task grows into a standalone workstream, `bd-tasks promote <task-id>`
creates a child bead, closes the dstask task, and links the new bead to the
parent via `bd update --parent`.

## Scope notes

- Shell script (bash), Chezmoi-managed alongside bd-timew.
- No dstask API — uses `dstask` CLI only.
- Follow-on bead J121-gc0 covers surfacing bead summaries in the morning
  dstask-notify toast.

title: Auto-session: Dolt-clone beads sandbox (Tier 3 — physical isolation)
description:
Physical-isolation variant of Dolt-backed beads sandboxing. Deferred — ship the Tier 1 (permission-deny) sibling first, and only return here if Tier 1 demonstrates insufficient containment in practice.

## Context

Discovered during the 2026-04-22 auto-session design conversation. Three tiers were identified:

- **Tier 1** (sibling, active): branch + permission-deny. Cheap, policy-enforced. Fine until a coordinator demonstrates ability to circumvent.
- **Tier 2** (sibling, research): `dolt sql-server` + scoped SQL user. Depends on bd supporting remote-server backend; needs discovery.
- **Tier 3** (this bead): physical DB isolation via `dolt clone` to a session-scoped path.

Tier 3 is the strongest containment — the coordinator's `.beads/` points at a completely separate on-disk Dolt DB; nothing it does can reach the canonical DB because they share no filesystem path. Merge-back is a `dolt remote add canonical file://<canonical-path>` + `dolt push` from the sandbox, then `dolt merge autosession/<slug>` on the canonical side.

## Why deferred

Three reasons to defer:

1. **Tier 1 is probably enough.** The bead mutation policy already constrains coordinator behavior prescriptively. The incremental value of physical isolation over permission-deny is only realized if the coordinator proves to be an adversarial actor — which it isn't; it's a model following its brief.
2. **Sync complexity.** Two Dolt DBs must be kept coherent, and the `bd sync`/export workflow would need awareness of the sandbox path. Tier 1 uses the native branch mechanism and requires no sync plumbing.
3. **Storage cost.** `dolt clone` duplicates the full DB history per session. For a bead DB with thousands of issues and long history, that's non-trivial per-session overhead. Tier 1 is storage-free (branches are cheap).

## Scope if undeferred

1. `--dolt-sandbox=physical` variant on `scripts/scaffold-autosession.sh`.
2. At session start: `dolt clone file://$(readlink -f .beads) <session-dir>/.beads-sandbox`.
3. Session's bd commands target `--db <session-dir>/.beads-sandbox/<db-name>.db`.
4. At session shutdown: `dolt remote add canonical file://<canonical>` + `dolt push canonical autosession/<slug>` from the sandbox.
5. On canonical: `dolt fetch` + `dolt merge autosession/<slug>` during `/import-session` approval.

## Trigger conditions for undeferral

Return to this bead if any of:

- A session demonstrates ability to bypass Tier 1 permission-deny (e.g., coordinator finds a way to invoke dolt via a subprocess the deny doesn't cover).
- Multiple concurrent auto-sessions need non-interfering bead DBs (Tier 1 branches *do* interfere on `.beads/HEAD`; Tier 3 DBs don't share state).
- Audit/compliance posture requires demonstrable physical isolation rather than policy-based containment.

## Estimate if undeferred

~6-8 hours. Clone + sync plumbing, `--db` routing discipline across all bd invocations in the session, merge-back flow, and additional `/import-session` UX for the "discard the clone" path.

## Status

**Deferred indefinitely** — see `--defer` field on this bead. Surface via `bd list --deferred` when evaluating whether to undefer.

comments.author: Michael Haynes
comments.text: Tier 2 research (J121-9kp.2.17.5, 2026-04-27) confirms Tier 3 (this bead) remains the only feasible full-isolation option right now. Two servers cannot share a data dir (Dolt exclusive write lock), and bd cannot connect to a branch-qualified database (validator rejects slashes). Physical clone is therefore the only path to true sandbox isolation without upstream bd changes. This bead remains correctly deferred until Tier 1 is live and real-world experience shows whether the stronger isolation is needed.

title: Auto-session: Dolt-branch beads sandbox (Tier 1 — permission-based)
description:
Ship the first and simplest tier of Dolt-backed auto-session beads sandboxing. Gives the user a pre-merge review window on whatever the coordinator did to bead state during a session, at low implementation cost.

## Context

Discovered during the 2026-04-22 auto-session design conversation (transcript at `~/Source/chezmoi-features/home/dot_claude/skills/auto-session/.transcripts/2026-04-22-autosession-design.jsonl`, turn-pair on the Dolt-as-sandbox follow-up). The existing bead mutation policy (`templates/beads/default.yaml`) constrains *how* the coordinator should mutate beads; Dolt branching adds the independent ability to *verify it did*. Those are orthogonal — the policy is prescriptive, the diff is descriptive.

## Scope

1. `--dolt-sandbox` flag on `scripts/scaffold-autosession.sh` (off by default).
2. When on, at session start:
   - `bd dolt commit -am "pre-autosession snapshot [<slug>]"` in `.beads/`
   - `dolt checkout -b autosession/<slug>` (bd uses the checked-out branch transparently)
3. Merge session-scoped permissions additions:
   - Deny `Bash(dolt:*)`, `Bash(bd dolt*)`, `Bash(bd sync*)`, `Bash(bd jira*)` — coordinator can mutate beads but not branches/remotes.
4. At session shutdown:
   - `bd dolt commit -am "end autosession [<slug>]"` on the autosession branch.
   - Leave the branch uncommitted-to-main for human review.
   - Emit a `dolt diff --json main autosession/<slug>` render to `<session-dir>/beads-diff.md` (small custom renderer; JSON → Markdown table grouped by table+row-action).
5. `/import-session` approval gate surfaces the diff alongside the existing exit-summary.
6. If approved: `dolt checkout main && dolt merge autosession/<slug>` inside `.beads/`; branch deleted. If rejected: branch retained for further review; `/import-session` offers a "discard branch" option that deletes it without merging.

## Out of scope (sibling beads)

- Physical isolation via `dolt clone` — sibling **Tier 3** bead, deferred until/unless Tier 1 proves insufficient.
- `dolt sql-server` + branch-pinned SQL user — sibling **research** bead; depends on bd supporting a remote-server backend, which is currently unknown.

## Verify

- Start a disposable `~/Source/_session-sandbox/` auto-session with `--dolt-sandbox`; confirm branch created, coordinator cannot run `dolt branch -D main`, beads mutations land on the autosession branch.
- Confirm `dolt diff main autosession/<slug>` at shutdown shows exactly the coordinator's changes and nothing else.
- Confirm `/import-session` can both merge and discard the branch.

## Pitfalls (already known)

- Permission-deny is tier-1 containment, not a hard sandbox. A coordinator willing to route through a shell script could still reach `dolt`. Policy-enforced, not mechanically enforced. Documented in the `--dolt-sandbox` flag's help text, not quietly glossed.
- `dolt diff` on a large schema can produce a lot of output. The renderer should group-by-table and cap rows-per-group before linking to the full JSON file.

## Estimate

~2-3 hours. Most of the work is the diff renderer; the branch + permission plumbing is thin.

comments.text: Tier 2 research (J121-9kp.2.17.5, 2026-04-27) confirms Tier 1 is the correct first step. Tier 2 (single server + sandbox branch) is feasible in principle but blocked at bd's database name validator — BEADS_DOLT_SERVER_DATABASE rejects slashes, so bd cannot connect to a branch-qualified database. An upstream bd --db-branch feature would unlock it. Until then, Tier 1 permission-deny is the only implementable sandbox that does not require physical clone cost. This bead is unblocked and should be shipped.

title: bd formula for incident / postmortem playbook
description:
# bd formula for incident / postmortem playbook

## What

Encode an incident-response playbook as a bd formula. One invocation — `bd mol pour incident --var severity=2 --var summary='<1-line>' --var surface=<portal|snowflake|pipeline|worker>` — spawns the standard response shape:

- **Anchor epic** — "Incident: {{summary}}" with `area:incident scope:local` + severity label.
- **Triage task** — first-response actions, dependency-free, claimable immediately.
- **Root-cause analysis task** — blocked-by triage. Notes field reserves space for investigation log.
- **Fix task** — blocked-by RCA. Parent of any fix sub-beads the responder may `bd create` ad-hoc during implementation.
- **Postmortem writeup task** — blocked-by fix. Targets `docs/postmortems/<ISO-date>-<slug>.md`.
- Optional per-severity variance: P0/P1 incidents also spawn a **communications task** (stakeholder updates) and a **dashboard-update task** (post-resolution entry in `docs/DASHBOARD.md`).

## Why

- Encodes tribal-knowledge response shape that otherwise lives in human memory or scattered references.
- Exercises the **severity-branching via `--var`** axis of bd formulas that plugin-doc and tooling-survey don't hit. If the schema supports conditional step inclusion (e.g., "step only if severity <= 1"), this bead validates that; if not, we learn the gap and document the workaround.
- Third canonical exemplar for the ecosystem — incident playbooks are a common ask and a good public-facing example.

## Why P3 (not P2)

Incidents are rare; cost of going without this formula is one hand-rolled `bd create` sequence per incident, and there haven't been so many that formalization is overdue. Ship the schema-discovery bead first, then pick this up. P2 would be justified if incident frequency rose; revisit then.

## Deliverables

- `.beads/formulas/incident.{json|yaml|toml}` (or `incident-postmortem.*` — choose based on what composes well if this ever gets extended).
- `.beads/formulas/incident.README.md` — variable reference (severity, summary, surface), how to invoke, how to close the anchor (all children done).
- **Severity-branching design note** in `memory/reference-bd-capabilities.md` § "bd formula" — documents whether conditional step inclusion is supported or requires workarounds.
- Optional: `docs/postmortems/_template.md` if the postmortem writeup step benefits from a document template (out of scope if the formula already covers it).

## Verify

- Dry-run `bd cook incident --var severity=2 --var summary='test' --var surface=portal --dry-run` and confirm correct step expansion.
- Dry-run `bd cook incident --var severity=0 ...` to confirm the communications + dashboard tasks appear in high-severity expansion.
- Full pour against a fictional scratch incident; walk the resulting bead tree; confirm `depends_on` edges (triage → RCA → fix → writeup) are correct.

## Dependencies

**Blocked by:** plugin-doc/worker-doc formula bead (shares schema-discovery cost).

**Sibling (no dep):** tooling-survey formula — different complexity axis; parallel once schema is known.

## Provenance

Child of epic `<formula-epic-id>`. Derives from recommendation #3 in the post-probe design conversation following `docs/reviews/beads-tooling-survey-2026-04-22.md`.

## Non-goals

- Not building a `/incident` slash command or skill in this bead. Follow-up once the formula exists.
- Not integrating with external paging / alerting (PagerDuty, ADO alerts). The formula is internal to bd; external wiring is a separate concern.

## Estimate

`est-h:2 est-p:1 est-t:S` — most of the cost is in the severity-branching design decision, which rides on the schema discovered in the blocking bead. If branching is natural, ~1.5h; if we need a workaround (spawn-all-then-close-low-sev), ~2.5h.
